rulings.Case Title: ISIDRO MIRANDA VS. ANDERSON ENTERPRISES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Penalties Sought As Part Of Settlement Pursuant To The Private Attorneys General Act (Paga), Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF ISIDRO MIRANDA's Motion To Approve Penalties Sought As Part Of Settlement Pursuant To The Private Attorneys General Act (Paga). Plaintiff Isidro Miranda's motion to approve PAGA settlement is granted. Although the motion papers do not fully address all of the issues raised by the court in response to the previous motion, most notably no time records are provided to enable the court to calculate a lodestar, sufficient information has been provided to warrant approving the settlement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Miranda is required to prepare a proposed order which grants the relief sought in the motion and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: THRIVENT FINANCIAL FOR LUTHERANS VS. BETTY YEE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Thrivent'S Memorandum Of Costs Or, In The Alternative, To Tax Costs Claimed By Thrivent; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT THE OFFICE OF THE STATE CONTROLLER, BETTY YEE's Motion To Strike Thrivent'S Memorandum Of Costs Or, In The Alternative, To Tax Costs Claimed By Thrivent. Defendants Betty Yee and the Office of the State Controller's motion to strike Thrivent memorandum of costs and alternative motion to tax costs claimed by Thrivent are denied in their entirety. Per CCP section 1032(a)(4), plaintiff Thrivent Financial for Lutherans is the prevailing party since it obtained a judgment favorable to it on the two asserted regulations that were the subject of this case. All of Thrivent's claimed costs are either allowable as a matter of right or in the court's discretion and, as to those claimed costs that are permitted in the exercise of the court's discretion, the court exercises its discretion to allow all such costs. All of the costs claimed by Thrivent were reasonably necessary for this case and are reasonable in amount. While the court at times questioned the parties regarding what was necessary for the resolution of this case, looking now in hindsight, the court does not believe that any of the costs incurred by Thrivent were unreasonably incurred. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Thrivent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MARC BRUNO VS. SAMUEL E BATY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Marc Bruno To Appear And Testify For The Completion Of Deposition For Award Of Monetary Sanctions Memorandum Of Points And Authroties Declaration Of Robert L Anderson In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 9, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANTS SAMUEL BATY, DINA SMITH'S Motion For Order Compelling Marc Bruno To Appear And Testify For The Completion Of Deposition For Award Of Monetary Sanctions Judge Pro Tem Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Judge Pro Tem. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Judge Pro Tem will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SABRINA MAREZ VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 14, Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. (part 2 of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only) While no published California decision has addressed whether the Express Drive rental program is a sufficient ground to impose liability against Lyft when the liability event occurred at a time when a driver was not on the Lyft platform, there is nothing about the Express Drive program that requires a different result than Le Elder reached with regard to the facts in that case where the driver was on call 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and was reimbursed for various vehicle maintenance expenses. Just as Le Elder rejected a "totality of circumstances" argument, plaintiffs' efforts to create a triable dispute by focusing on the Express Drive program must fail. (Le Elder, 21 Cal. App. 4th at 1609 ("The totality of circumstances is of no legal significance where, as here, an employee is engaged in purely personal activity when his or her tort causes injury to a third party.")). Similarly, although Mr. Gaurano's misunderstanding regarding the extent of his insurance coverage in the Express Drive rental program may be attributable to Lyft's lack of clarity, that is not a basis for imposing vicarious liability against Lyft for an accident that occurred outside Mr. Gaurano's relationship with Lyft. Because the undisputed facts show that Mr. Gaurano acted outside the scope of his relationship with Lyft when the accident occurred, as a matter of law Lyft is not vicariously liable for Mr. Gaurano's alleged negligence under an employment, agency, or joint venture theory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lyft is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: SABRINA MAREZ VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 14, Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. (part 1 of 2) Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment as to plaintiff Marissa Cruz's complaint is granted. Le Elder v. Rice (1994) 21 Cal.App.4th 1604, which has been cited approvingly numerous times and most recently in 2013 and has never been criticized or overruled, remains good law and is on-point authority requiring the granting of this motion. The "coming and going" rule does not apply because defendant Jonathan Gaurano was returning home from working at a conference unrelated to Lyft when the accident happened and had not activated the Lyft platform at any point that day. Because the coming and going rule is inapplicable, per Le Elder, this court may not employ a "benefit to the employer" analysis, which is urged by both plaintiffs. (Le Elder, 21 Cal. App. 4th at 1608 ("The trial court mistakenly applied a 'benefit to the employer' analysis. It may have been referring to the benefit-to-the-employer exception to the 'coming and going' rule, but that rule is inapt here.") (citation omitted)). As with the employee in Le Elder, Mr. Gaurano was engaged in a "purely personal" matter at the time the accident occurred. Mr. Gaurano was returning home to eat after working at a conference unrelated to Lyft and the employee in Le Elder was returning home to eat after dropping his kids off at school. (Id.) The primary consideration is not whether the vehicle can be fairly considered a company vehicle or whether Lyft benefitted from Mr. Gaurano driving it for personal reasons, but whether Mr. Gaurano was acting within the scope of the alleged employment, agency agreement, or joint venture when the accident happened. (Id. at 1609 ("The existence of nonexistence of employer benefits is not dispositive in determining vicarious liability. Rather, the dispositive factor is whether an employee was acting within the scope of employment when the tort was committed.") (citation omitted)). None of the cases relied on by the plaintiffs are to the contrary. Lazar v. Thermal Equip. Corp. (1983) 148 Cal.App.3d 458, 463, 465-67 applied an exception to the coming and going rule because the driver's deviation from the scope of employment was minimal. Sumrall v. Modern Alloys, Inc. (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 961 reversed a trial court's grant of summary judgment because it was a question of fact whether the driver was on a "business errand" when the accident occurred. Unlike the driver in Lobo v. Tamco (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 297, Mr. Gaurano was not on call for customer requests and could not have been unless he activated the Lyft platform. (Lobo, 182 Cal. App. 4th at 301-03 (holding that because the driver drove his vehicle home with an expectation that he might need to respond in-person to customer complaints, the driver was still acting within the scope of employment and vicarious liability was appropriate)).(end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HK)

(Le Elder, 21 Cal. App. 4th at 1609 ("The totality of circumstances is of no legal significance where, as here, an employee is engaged in purely personal activity when his or her tort causes injury to a third party.")). Similarly, although Mr. Gaurano's misunderstanding regarding the extent of his insurance coverage in the Express Drive rental program may be attributable to Lyft's lack of clarity, that is not a basis for imposing vicarious liability against Lyft for an accident that occurred outside Mr. Gaurano's relationship with Lyft. Because the undisputed facts show that Mr. Gaurano acted outside the scope of his relationship with Lyft when the accident occurred, as a matter of law Lyft is not vicariously liable for Mr. Gaurano's alleged negligence under an employment, agency, or joint venture theory. Le Elder v. Rice (1994) 21 Cal.App.4th 1604, which has been cited approvingly numerous times and most recently in 2013 and has never been criticized or overruled, remains good law and is on-point authority requiring the granting of this motion. The "coming and going" rule does not apply because defendant Jonathan Gaurano was returning home from working at a conference unrelated to Lyft when the accident happened and had not activated the Lyft platform at any point that day. Because the coming and going rule is inapplicable, per Le Elder, this court may not employ a "benefit to the employer" analysis, which is urged by both plaintiffs. (Le Elder, 21 Cal. App. 4th at 1608 ("The trial court mistakenly applied a 'benefit to the employer' analysis. It may have been referring to the benefit-to-the-employer exception to the 'coming and going' rule, but that rule is inapt here.") (citation omitted)). As with the employee in Le Elder, Mr. Gaurano was engaged in a "purely personal" matter at the time the accident occurred. Mr. Gaurano was returning home to eat after working at a conference unrelated to Lyft and the employee in Le Elder was returning home to eat after dropping his kids off at school. (Id.) The primary consideration is not whether the vehicle can be fairly considered a company vehicle or whether Lyft benefitted from Mr. Gaurano driving it for personal reasons, but whether Mr. Gaurano was acting within the scope of the alleged employment, agency agreement, or joint venture when the accident happened. (Id. at 1609 ("The existence of nonexistence of employer benefits is not dispositive in determining vicarious liability. Rather, the dispositive factor is whether an employee was acting within the scope of employment when the tort was committed.") (citation omitted)). None of the cases relied on by the plaintiffs are to the contrary. Lazar v. Thermal Equip. Corp. (1983) 148 Cal.App.3d 458, 463, 465-67 applied an exception to the coming and going rule because the driver's deviation from the scope of employment was minimal. Sumrall v. Modern Alloys, Inc. (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 961 reversed a trial court's grant of summary judgment because it was a question of fact whether the driver was on a "business errand" when the accident occurred. Unlike the driver in Lobo v. Tamco (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 297, Mr. Gaurano was not on call for customer requests and could not have been unless he activated the Lyft platform. (Lobo, 182 Cal. App. 4th at 301-03 (holding that because the driver drove his vehicle home with an expectation that he might need to respond in-person to customer complaints, the driver was still acting within the scope of employment and vicarious liability was appropriate)). While no published California decision has addressed whether the Express Drive rental program is a sufficient ground to impose liability against Lyft when the liability event occurred at a time when a driver was not on the Lyft platform, there is nothing about the Express Drive program that requires a different result than Le Elder reached with regard to the facts in that case where the driver was on call 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and was reimbursed for various vehicle maintenance expenses. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ASHLEY RUST VS. SENDHUB, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Sealing Order And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF ASHLEY RUST, GARRETT JOHNSON, RYAN PFEFFER, JOHN FALLONE's Motion For A Sealing Order. Plaintiffs' motion for a sealing order regarding the confidential mutual general release is denied. Even assuming that the plaintiffs have adequately shown that there is an overriding interest in keeping the release or portions thereof from the public, plaintiffs have not shown that there is a substantial probability that the overriding interest would be prejudiced if this motion is denied. rulings.Case Title: LVOVICH & SZUCSKO, P.C. A CALIFORNIA PROFESSIONAL VS. YURI GALASHOV ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Set Aside Order Granting X-Defendant Lvovich & Szucsko, P.C.'S Motion To Strike X-Complaint From January 31, 18
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT YURI GALASHOV's Motion To Set Aside Order Granting X-Defendant Lvovich & Szucsko, P.C.'S Motion To Strike X-Complaint From January 31, 18. Cross-complainant Yuri Galashov's motion to set aside the January 31, 2018 order striking his cross-complaint is denied. Even assuming that Mr. Galashov did not receive notice of the hearing on the motion seeking to strike his cross-complaint, his lack of diligence in bringing this motion and doing nothing to prosecute his cross-complaint warrants denial of this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Lvovich & Szucsko is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: LESLIE LAPAYOWKER VS. AIRBNB,INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 9, PLAINTIFF LESLIE LAPAYOWKER's Motion For Protective Order. Off calendar in light of the September 27, 2018 order setting reinstating the default of defendant Carlos Del Olmo. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: DANICA MORAIS VS. SAN FRANCISCO MUNICIPAL TRANSPORTATION AGENCY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And Further Responses And To Strike Defendant'S Objections To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents - Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 9, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF DANICA MORAIS' Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And Further Responses And To Strike Defendant'S Objections To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents - Set One Judge Pro Tem Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Judge Pro Tem. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Judge Pro Tem will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: INS COMM STATE OF CA VS WESTERN EMPLOYER
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Approval Of Settlement Agreement And Notice Of Hearing On Motion
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 9, 2018, Line 12, PETITIONER INSURANCE COMMISSIONER OF THE STATE OF CALIF.'s Motion For Approval Of Settlement Agreement. Applicant Insurance Comissioner's motion for approval of settlement agreement with claimant Aluminum Shapes is granted. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Insurance Commissioner is required to prepare a proposed order which grants the relief sought in the motion and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)
